item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired. Edits made as described below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, a big part of this article is its testing with D-class. Turns out it has also been mentioned for testing with other anomalous objects, including SCP-1497 and SCP-2486. It would likely that permission for this kind of testing would be mentioned in the containment procedures, as such I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

All samples of SCP-1528 are to be kept in air-tight containers under restricted access at Site-77 in Storage Locker 662. Approval of the project command authority is required for testing. When in containment, SCP-1528 is not to be stored in containers with any complex or easily broken opening mechanisms. When being tested, a small amount should be stored in an aerosol dispersal system.

To this:

All samples of SCP-1528 are to be kept in air-tight containers under restricted access at Site-77 in Storage Locker 662. Approval of the project command authority is required for testing. When in containment, SCP-1528 is not to be stored in containers with any complex or easily broken opening mechanisms. When being tested, a small amount should be stored in an aerosol dispersal system. Testing of SCP-1528 with additional anomalous objects has been approved on a case by case basis.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object flaw: None inherent; before working for the SCP Foundation, Dr. Boyd, who is not a physicist, had expressed cynicism concerning special relativity.

Perhaps the word you're looking for here is "skepticism"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, this really intrigues me as a musician. Mainly the test result when they played a song at double speed. It's just that there are so many things you can do to music to make it "wrong", you could play a wrong note, you could change the key, you could even change the time signature (I once heard of my school band playing a prank on some cheerleaders by modifying a song they were supposed to dance to to be in 7/4 instead of 4/4). Part of me wonders, if this were real, how would people react to such changes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what this needs is less attempt to explain outright what it does, in that case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way the item is described implies that the effects disappear when someone passes out. Why did the one guy die after he passed out? Shouldn't his body have started to turn back to normal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the description of SCP-914; just the description. It's surprisingly ambiguous. Like 914, this is one that pretty much must be explained through example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation has good taste in music.

That said, I dunno, this is a little squidgey from where I see it. (As in, it's really hard to describe what's going on scientifically. Also, the description led me to believe the effects were completely different from what ended up being the case.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that the music used is TMBG, as this ensures there are no flaws with the music itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a test log here: Experiment Log 1528

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Test ideas for the log:

Expose the subject to a Dali paiting
A Coke bottle with Pepsi inside
A kit-kat bar that was bitten sideways

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1528 is a blue liquid similar in appearance to commercial bleach-based cleaning products.

I thought blue window cleaner was typically ammonia based. Is this deliberate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My guess is that it would make him perceive himself and everyone else as lumbering giants.

I like the idea that it would affect the growth of a child, but unfortunately the effect only lasts until unconsciousness which probably wouldn't be long enough to mess it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true! Bathtubs make for a nice clinical white backdrop. Looks like my three dollar investment on Windex was well spent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that the picture here was taken by llama66613, in his bathtub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If any object is perceived as flawed or broken…

Not wrong, but perceivable as flawed. (We could of course instruct him to make fallacious statements during the test, which would make him wrong.) Because of the temperature test, I was wondering if a dwarf would actually affect the size of the subject, but after reading llama66613's explanation I I guess it might only affect perception of people's size (unless the subject is a small child, in which case it might affect it's further development??).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You saying dwarves are wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Work out a viable entry first, then find a free number for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, body temperature is regulated by Preoptic area of the Hypothalamus1, so because the brain thought it was cold, it turned up its thermostat, basically.

Footnotes
1. Which I discovered from 60 seconds googling

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like your second suggestion and I think it'd be strong enough to be a final test on this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The score seemed to miss a +1 so I added one.

subject will undergo a sensory or cognitive shift that is applied to their entire consciousness

From my understanding, it seems to be more then just cognitive, if it can actually raise body temperature.

Some ideas of test:
What if subject is put in presence of a dwarf?
What if the subject already has a mental disorder that makes them think the world is wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever effect but that last test knocks it out of the park.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'The Eye of the Beholder' anyone?

Anyone…?

okay then :P

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely loved Addendum 2.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhh, of course. I'm not sure why I didn't think of selective perspection fuckery.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Their sense of color was inverted apart from the painting. It's the same reason why the illustration on the wall didn't get moved down another 20 degrees once the effect was initiated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone's ability to see color was inverted and their memories fucked with, the painting would still look wrong to them, right? That's the part that I'm stalling on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something like that could be interesting. Tests for something like this are quite easy to make entertaining and fresh so I'd definitely be open to the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm considering adding a collaborative testing log to the end if people give me good test ideas. So far, all I got is "listening to music backwards" and I want to get at least five before making a log

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the originality! Well done. My only criticism might be that I'd like to see it end with a test that might impact me a little more with a feeling of weirdness. Other than that great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so I can no longer go for this one, eh? Well, my entry needed a lot of improving anyways, so it matters not. I'll just seek out a new number to go for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humans are inherently flawed and disgusting and wrong hahaha yes am I this D-class?

Clever but no vote form me. It doesn't strike me as interesting beyond its vaguely clever/amusing log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A collaboration between me and Llama66613
